title: Vagish Hemmige, MD MS
text:
Overview
Professor of Medicine (Infectious Diseases) at Albert Einstein College of Medicine and transplant infectious diseases physician at Montefiore Einstein in the Bronx, NY. Interests include HIV-to-HIV thoracic transplantation, HIV prevention and treatment, and building open R tools for transplant data research.

Current roles: Professor, Medicine (ID), Albert Einstein College of Medicine; Infectious Disease physician, Montefiore Medical Center
Location: Bronx, New York

Clinical interests: Transplant ID, HIV care & prevention, opportunistic infections

Methods/tech: R, Quarto, Arrow/DuckDB, reproducible pipelines

title: Vagish Hemmige, MD MS
text:
Highlights

Part of Montefiore-Einstein’s immunocompromised transplant (ICT) ID team

Medical degree from NYU Grossman School of Medicine; >10 years practice experience.

Faculty/clinician listings and contact available via Einstein/Montefiore directories.

title: Vagish Hemmige, MD MS
text:

sRtr — Tools for working with UNOS/SRTR transplant data in R

usRds — Tools for working with the United States Renal Data system Repo/docs: https://vagishhemmige.github.io/usRds/

title: About
text:
Vagish Hemmige
I work at the intersection of infectious diseases and transplantation, with a focus on HIV care & prevention, HIV-to-HIV thoracic transplantation, and building open, reproducible R workflows for large healthcare datasets.
I’m based in the Bronx, New York. Day to day, I split time between patient care, program building, and collaborative research—especially projects that improve access to transplant for people living with HIV and make complex data usable by clinical teams.

title: About
text:
What I’m working on

Transplant & HIV: advancing pathways for HIV-positive donor thoracic transplants and sharing practical “how-to” resources for new programs.
Reproducible analytics in R: extensive experience using R to analyze clinical data.
Education: clinical reasoning, and bringing data skills to clinicians with approachable examples.

title: About
text:
Open-source projects

sRtr — R helpers for working with UNOS/SRTR transplant data
https://vagishhemmige.github.io/sRtr/
strobe — STROBE-style inclusion/exclusion tracking for observational studies
https://vagishhemmige.github.io/strobe/
usRds (in progress) — utilities for loading, labeling, and converting USRDS claims files to Parquet with schema-aware helpers.

title: About
text:
Selected interests & methods

Clinical: transplant ID, opportunistic infections, HIV prevention & primary care

Research: HIV and thoracic transplant, big data work with the SRTR and UNOS

Teaching: have taught clinical infectious diseases to trainees as well as statistics and epidemiology.

title: About
text:
Speaking & teaching
I enjoy guest lectures, workshops, and small-group sessions on:

Practical R for clinicians and researchers
Epidemiology and biostatistics
Transplant ID topics (HIV D+/R+ programs, protocols, safety)

title: About
text:
Mentoring
I have mentored trainees in case reports, clinical research, and big data/research methods.
I am open to mentoring trainees and faculty at other institutions who are interested in working with big data sets such as the USRDS or the SRTR and performing their own analyses in R. While a remote trainee would need to obtain their own copy of the data, Github would enable collaboration on the analysis code.

title: Mentoring
text:
I have mentored trainees in case reports, clinical case series, and big data/research methods.
I am open to mentoring trainees and faculty at other institutions who are interested in working with big data sets such as the USRDS or the SRTR and performing their own analyses in R. While a remote trainee would need to obtain their own copy of the data, we can collaborate via GitHub on the analysis.
Message me via GitHub if you are interested in collaborating remotely.

title: Mentoring
text:
I have mentored trainees in case reports, clinical case series, and big data/research methods.
I am open to mentoring trainees and faculty at other institutions who are interested in working with big data sets such as the USRDS or the SRTR and performing their own analyses in R. While a remote trainee would need to obtain their own copy of the data, we can collaborate via GitHub on the analysis.
Message me via GitHub if you are interested in collaborating remotely.

title: Mentoring
text:
Active Mentees

Hannah Rosenthal
Medical student
Mentorship role: Primary research mentor
Current position: MS4 at Albert Einstein College of Medicine
Years: 2025-2026
Hannah is applying her R programming skills to perform an analysis of the SRTR database, demonstrating that people living with HIV have equivalent outcomes to people without HIV after simultaneous liver-kidney transplant. She also took the lead to present these results and will lead manuscript preparation.
Projects

HIV-SLK Outcomes
HOPE-CDC Analysis 2026

Abstracts

Nationwide UNOS Analysis of Mortality After Simultaneous Liver–Kidney Transplantation in Patients With HIV, 2005–2023.
NYC Center for AIDS Research Symposium 2026 · New York, NY
Geographic Access to Liver Transplant Centers with HIV and HOPE Experience, 2017–2022.
IDWeek 2025 · Atlanta, GA




Morgan Marc
Medical student
Mentorship role: Primary research mentor
Current position: MS1 at Albert Einstein College of Medicine
Years: 2025-2026
Morgan is interested in thoracic transplant in people living with HIV and improving referral patterns for this vulnerable population.
Projects

HOPE-CDC Analysis 2026

Anagha reviewed 100 published papers using the United States Renal Data System, demonstrating the low proportion of analyses utilizing claims or cost components of the database. Her work has been presented at American Transplant Congress.

title: Mentoring
text:
Former Mentees

Miguel Cervera Hernandez
Fellow
Mentorship role: Primary research mentor
Current position: Attending at Sanford Health
Years: 2020-2021
Profile link
Dr. Cervera used STATA to analyze outcomes after the inpatient diagnosis of cryptococcal infection in kidney transplant recipients, utilizing the United States Renal Data System. He took the lead for the statistical analysis and poster presentation components of the project. His code was adapted into R for use in the usRds package.
Manuscripts

High Mortality and Associated Risk Factors in Kidney Transplant Recipients With Cryptococcosis-A Nationwide Cohort Study Over a Decade Using USRDS Data



Jonathan Czeresnia
Fellow
Mentorship role: Primary research mentor
Current position: Attending at Rutgers Health
Years: 2021-2022
Profile link
During his senior year of the ID fellowship, Dr. Czeresnia learned R programming via a Coursera course. He used those skills to perform an analysis of the SRTR database, demonstrating that people living with HIV have equivalent outcomes to people without HIV after lung transplant. He also took the lead to present these results at American Transplant Congress.
Manuscripts

Proof of Concept That Implementation of a Multispecialty Educational Intervention May Improve Acceptance of Organs from Donors with HIV at a Tertiary Care Hospital: A Single-Center Study

Lung Transplantation Outcomes in Recipients Living with HIV in the United States.

Transplantation of solid organs recovered from deceased donors recently infected by SARS-CoV-2 in the United States.
Infectious Diseases Society of New York Annual Symposium 2022 · New York, NY
Implementation of a medical education intervention to improve acceptance of HIV-positive donors for transplant at a tertiary care hospital.
IDWeek 2022 · Washington, DC
